4: hineh afela lo yoshra nafsho bo vetzaddik beemunato yichyeh: 'Behold, his soul is puffed up; it is not upright within him, but the righteous shall live by his faith [or faithfulness, emunah].' This verse is the most quoted OT text in the NT letters. The Hebrew emunah covers a semantic range from 'faithfulness' (reliability, steadfastness) to 'faith' (trust, belief). Paul's use in Romans and Galatians emphasizes the trust/belief aspect; the Habakkuk context emphasizes endurance and faithfulness during the Babylonian crisis. Both senses are present: the righteous person is characterized by trust in YHWH's promise that sustains them through the crisis (both the original Babylonian threat and the ongoing existential challenge of life under divine wrath deferred). The Reformers' rediscovery of this verse (Luther: the just shall live by faith, not by works of the law) was the theological engine of the Protestant Reformation.
1: al mishmarti e'emod vaetiyatzevah al matzor: 'I will take my stand at my watchpost and station myself on the tower.' Habakkuk's posture between complaints and answers is watchman-theology: mishmar (watch/guard post, H4931) and matzor (watchtower, H4692) are military observation positions. The prophet-as-watchman is developed in Ezekiel 3:17 and Habakkuk 33:7 — the prophet stationed to warn the people. Here Habakkuk stations himself to receive YHWH's response. Mah yadaber bi umai ashiv al tokachti — 'to see what he will say to me, and what I will answer concerning my complaint.' The word tokachti (my reproof/complaint, H8433) acknowledges that the prophet's complaint may itself require a divine correction.
2: vaya'aneni YHWH vayomer kotov chazon vevaer al haluachot: 'And the LORD answered me: Write the vision; make it plain on tablets, so he may run who reads it.' The divine response begins with an inscription command: write (ketov), make plain (baer, H874 = to engrave/inscribe clearly). Al haluachot (on tablets) — the same material as the stone tablets of the Ten Commandments (Exodus 24:12; Habakkuk 32:15-16), suggesting this vision has Torah-like permanence and authority. Lemaan yarutz qore vo — 'so that he may run who reads it' — the inscription is meant to be readable at a glance by a runner (messenger on the road), or perhaps the phrase means 'the reader of it should run' — flee or act urgently on what is written.
3: ki od chazon lamoled veyfuach laqetz velo yechazev: 'For still the vision awaits its appointed time; it hastens to the end — it will not lie.' The theology of delayed fulfillment: moled (appointed time/birth-moment, H4150 — the same root as moed, the fixed appointed feast times) indicates that divine fulfillment comes at the time YHWH has precisely set. Im yitmahmeha chakeh lo — 'if it seems slow, wait for it; it will surely come, it will not delay.' The author of Hebrews quotes v3-4 in Habakkuk 10:37-38 as encouragement to endure: 'For yet a little while, and the coming one will come and will not delay.' The 'coming one' is Christ — Habakkuk's 'vision awaiting its appointed time' becomes the pattern for the parousia.
5: veaf ki hayayin boged gever yahir velo yinaveh: 'Moreover, wine is a traitor, an arrogant man who is never at rest.' The woe-cycle (Habakkuk 2:6-20) is introduced by a general statement about the proud and greedy. Hayin boged (wine is a traitor) — wine as the metaphor for Babylon's insatiable appetite (cf. Jeremiah 51:7: Babylon is a gold cup in YHWH's hand that made all the earth drunk). Asher hirchiv kishol nafsho vehu kamavet velo yisba — 'who enlarges his desire like Sheol, and like death he never has enough.' The comparison to Sheol and death for imperial appetite is striking: Sheol (the underworld) is never satisfied (Proverbs 27:20; Habakkuk 30:15-16) — the empire that keeps consuming nations is compared to the realm of death's insatiability.
6: halo eleh kulo mashal alav yisa umelitzah chidot lo yomar: 'Shall not all these take up their taunt against him, with scoffing and riddles for him, and say...' The five woes of Habakkuk 2 (vv6, 9, 12, 15, 19) are structured as a taunt-song (mashal, H4912) — a wisdom form in which the tables are turned on the oppressor. Hoy hamarbeh lo asher ad matai — 'Woe to him who heaps up what is not his own — for how long? — and loads himself with pledges.' The first woe addresses economic exploitation through debt-pledge (avitit, H5671 = thick clay = heavy debt). The rhetorical question 'for how long?' echoes Habakkuk's own complaint in Habakkuk 1:2.
7: halo petom yaqumu nashecheicha veqatzu mezazeicha vehayita limshisot lamo: 'Will not your debtors suddenly arise, and those who make you tremble wake up? Then you will be their prey.' The reversal: the creditor becomes the debtor, the oppressor becomes the prey. Nashecheicha (your creditors/biters, from nashach = to bite/charge interest, H5391) — the bitter word-play: those who took interest ('biters') will themselves rise up against their oppressor. The economic exploitation by debt will produce the very rebellion that destroys the empire.
8: ki atta shallota goyim rabbim yishalluchacha kol yeter amim: 'Because you have plundered many nations, all the remnant of the peoples shall plunder you.' The lex talionis at the international scale: the nation that plundered will be plundered (shallota... yishalluchacha — same root shalal). Midam adam uchamas eretz qiryah vekhol yoshevei vah — 'for the blood of man and violence to the earth, to cities and all who dwell in them.' The three charges — blood, violence to land, violence to cities — are the comprehensive indictment of imperial conquest. Chamas (violence) appears again: the same sin Habakkuk complained about in Judah (Habakkuk 1:2-3) is now the grounds for Babylon's judgment.
9: hoy botze betzah ra leveito lashum bamarom qinno: 'Woe to him who gets evil gain for his house, to set his nest on high, to be safe from the reach of harm!' The second woe addresses economic exploitation for self-protection. Betzah ra (evil gain/unjust profit, H1214 + H7451) — the term batzah covers both cutting/slicing and profiteering (Jeremiah 6:13: from prophet to priest, every one deals falsely / profits unjustly). Lashum bamarom qinno — 'to set his nest on high' — the eagle-nest image (cf. Obad 4) for impregnable security through wealth. The woe declares this strategy ultimately futile.
10: yatzata bosheth lebeitecha qetzot amim rabbim: 'You have devised shame for your house by cutting off many peoples.' The verb yatzata (you have counseled/devised, H3289) — the planning that was intended for security (lashum bamarom, v9) has actually produced shame. Bosheth (shame/disgrace, H1322) is the reversal of the honor (kavod) the empire sought. Vechoteh nafsho — 'and have forfeited your life' — the soul (nefesh) lost in the pursuit of security for the house (bayit). The irony: seeking to protect the house by exploiting nations, you destroy both the house and yourself.
11: ki even mizir tizaq vekaphis metz aneha: 'For the stone will cry out from the wall, and the beam from the woodwork respond.' The building materials of the house built on unjust gain will testify against its builder. This is the language of creation as moral witness: even inanimate materials have a voice to accuse. The NT parallel: Luke 19:40 (the stones would cry out), where Jesus applies creation-witness language to the acclaim due to the messianic king entering Jerusalem. Romans 8:19-22 (creation groaning) develops the same theme of creation testifying against human sin.
12: hoy boneh ir bedamim vekonen qiryah beavel: 'Woe to him who builds a town with blood and founds a city on iniquity!' The third woe addresses the city built on violence. Damim (blood, H1818) and avel (iniquity/wickedness, H5766) are the twin foundations of the imperial city. The specific application to Babylon (whose construction projects used forced labor from conquered peoples) is historically precise. Jeremiah 51:58 repeats the charge: 'Babylon's broad walls shall be leveled to the ground, and her high gates shall be burned with fire; the peoples labor for nothing.' The city that seems impregnable is built on morally unstable ground.
13: halo hineh me'et YHWH tzvaot veyig'u amim bedei esh velaomim bedei rik yia'efu: 'Behold, is it not from the LORD of hosts that peoples labor merely for fire, and nations weary themselves for nothing?' The woe is grounded in YHWH's sovereign irony: the labor of the nations in service of Babylon is ultimately purposeless — ledei esh (for fire) and ledei rik (for nothing/emptiness). All of Babylon's building projects will be consumed by the same fire their conquest brought on others. YHWH tzvaot (LORD of hosts) — the military title underscores that the divine commander outranks the Babylonian empire commander.
14: ki timale ha'aretz ladaat et kevod YHWH kamayim yechassu al hayam: 'For the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD as the waters cover the sea.' This verse, the centerpiece of the woe-cycle, is the positive eschatological vision that grounds all the judgments: YHWH's glory will ultimately fill the earth as universally as water fills the ocean. The parallel in Isaiah 11:9 (the messianic kingdom) and Numbers 14:21 (YHWH's oath) confirms this is an eschatological absolute. Da'at et kevod YHWH (knowledge of the glory of the LORD) — not merely intellectual knowledge but experiential recognition of YHWH's presence and character filling all creation.
15: hoy mashqeh reahuhu mesapech chamato af shaker: 'Woe to him who makes his neighbors drink — you pour out your wrath and make them drunk, in order to gaze at their nakedness.' The fourth woe uses the cup of intoxicating wine as a metaphor for imperial humiliation of conquered peoples. Mesapech chamato (pouring out his wrath/poison) — chamah (H2534) covers both wrath and the heat of wine/poison. The connection to the cup of YHWH's wrath (Jeremiah 25:15-17; Revelation 14:10; Habakkuk 16:19) is deliberate: Babylon forces the cup on others; YHWH will force the cup on Babylon.
16: savata qalon mekavod shete gam atta vehare'el: 'You will have your fill of shame instead of glory. Drink, yourself, and show your uncircumcision!' The reversal of the fourth woe: the one who made others drink will drink himself. Qalon mekavod (shame instead of glory) — the kavod-qalon antithesis is fundamental to biblical theology: the one who seeks glory through shame-infliction will receive shame. Tassov aleicha kos yamin YHWH — 'the cup in the LORD's right hand will come around to you' — the eschatological cup of judgment that the oppressor has been forcing on others will turn and be forced on the oppressor.
17: ki chamas Levanon yechassecha veshood behemot yechitelan: 'The violence done to Lebanon will overwhelm you, and the destruction of the beasts will terrify you.' The environmental dimension of the woe: the Babylonians plundered the cedar forests of Lebanon (confirmed in Babylonian royal inscriptions — Nebuchadnezzar boasts of bringing cedar from Lebanon). Chamas Levanon (violence to Lebanon) — the deforestation and ecological destruction wrought by imperial logging and building projects. The animal kingdom (behemot) destroyed by the Babylonians will haunt them. The same charge appears in vv8 and 12 — blood, violence, destruction — forming a refrain of ecological and humanitarian indictment.
18: mah ho'il pesel ki pesalo yotzero massekhah umorah shaqer: 'What profit is an idol when its maker has shaped it, a metal image, a teacher of lies?' The fifth woe (vv18-20) addresses idolatry directly. The polemic against idols uses the standard wisdom argument: the idol is pesel (carved image, H6459) shaped by its maker (yotzero) — the creator of the idol is human, therefore the idol is not god. More shaqer (teacher of lies, H8267) — the idol teaches what is false about ultimate reality. The idol-maker trusts in his own product (vatach yotzero alav) — the ultimate futility: trusting in something you yourself made.
19: hoy omer laaetz haqitza uri leeven dumam: 'Woe to him who says to a wooden thing, Awake; to a silent stone, Arise!' The fifth woe is the woe against the idol. Calling a piece of wood or stone to 'awake' (haqitza) and 'arise' (uri) — the same vocabulary used to awaken YHWH himself in the psalms of lament (Psalms 44:23; Habakkuk 78:65) — is applied to the mute idol. The idol is hua yoru (it will teach) — but covered in gold and silver, it has no breath (ruach ein beqirbo). The ruach (spirit/breath/wind) that animates everything is absent from the idol. 'There is no breath in it' is the ultimate verdict on all idolatry: what is worshipped is inanimate.
20: vaYHWH beheikhal qodsho has mikol haeretz lefanav: 'But the LORD is in his holy temple; let all the earth keep silence before him.' The great contrast to the five woes and the lifeless idols: YHWH is in his holy temple (heikhal qodsho) — the earthly temple (Jerusalem) as the throne-room of the cosmic king. Has mikol haeretz lefanav — 'hush before him, all the earth.' The verb has (hush/silence, H2013) demands a reverential silence that is the opposite of idol-worship's futile appeals. The verse is the hinge of the book: from the noise of complaint and woe to the silence of awe before the enthroned YHWH. Zephaniah 1:7 and Zechariah 2:13 use the same formula. The universe falls silent before the one who alone is truly alive and sovereign.
